title: Is the most random pattern random? Maximizing localization in a two-dimensional lattice with engineered disorder
abstract: We investigate localization in two models: a single particle in a two-dimensional square lattice described by the tight binding Hamiltonian, and a two-dimensional square qubit lattice. It is well-known that Anderson localization occurs under suitable conditions in which the system parameters are chosen randomly from some statistical distribution. We propose a situation in which the parameters, specifically the on-site energies, are carefully chosen in such a way that a localization-quantifying parameter is maximized. We demonstrate the optimization procedure with numerical calculations in which the engineered localization significantly exceeds the average localization caused by a random distribution of the on-site energies. We explore the relation between spatial patterns and localization efficiency. Furthermore, we use perturbation theory to gain insight into the localization mechanism and obtain an improved cost function for optimization calculations, leading to enhanced localization in both the single-particle and full Hilbert spaces. Although large-scale simulations for qubit lattices are computationally infeasible, we use small-system simulations to demonstrate that results obtained using the single-particle tight binding model can be adapted to identify optimal settings for qubit lattice systems to achieve maximum decoupling between the qubits, which can be valuable for optimizing the idle-state settings on a quantum processor.
summary: This work explores engineering disorder in lattice systems to achieve maximal quantum state localization. By optimizing on-site energies, the authors show that engineered patterns outperform random disorder, and crucially, they demonstrate that the optimal single-particle settings can guide the design of highly decoupled qubit processors.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether engineered disorder can achieve stronger quantum state localization than purely random disorder in lattice systems.
main_result: Optimizing on-site energies to maximize localization in the single-particle model successfully identifies optimal settings that achieve maximum decoupling in the complex, many-body qubit lattice.
method: The authors use optimization techniques (like genetic algorithms) on cost functions derived from autocorrelation functions and perturbation theory to suppress quantum transport.
model_or_system: The study analyzes two models: a single particle on a 2D square lattice using a tight-binding Hamiltonian, and a two-dimensional square qubit lattice relevant for quantum processors.
key_observables: Site-averaged autocorrelation function C(t) (localization measure) and the cost function L_FHS (for qubit decoupling).
important_parameters: On-site energies {W_i}, hopping amplitudes J_ij, and the detuning parameters $\Delta_{ij} = |W_i - W_j|$.
main_assumptions: The single-particle optimization constraints are assumed to be sufficient to guide the optimization for the exponentially complex full qubit Hilbert space.
figures_summary: Figures compare the time evolution of the autocorrelation function C(t) for random, optimized, and regular lattice configurations, demonstrating superior localization in the engineered cases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing single-particle localization using cost functions, to applying these concepts to the full qubit lattice dynamics, and finally refining the optimization using perturbation theory to account for higher-order hopping processes.
why_interesting: The methodology of mapping complex many-body localization problems onto simpler, solvable single-particle optimization problems is highly valuable for designing quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time evolution of the autocorrelation function (Eq. (2)) for different sets of on-site energies Wi on a 3×3 lattice. Panels (a) correspond to random on-site parameters, (b) to optimized lattice, (c) to the perfect chessboard lattice, and (d) to the perturbed chessboard lattice (see text). For each configuration, the upper panels (x.1) show the time dependant autocorrelation function for the single-particle case, the middle panels (x.2) display the corresponding lattice structure, and the lower panels (x.3) present the time dependant autocorrelation function for the FHS. The optimization procedure is performed at the single- particle level by minimizing the cost function defined in...

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the autocorrelation function (Eq. (2)) for different sets of on-site energies Wi on a 3 × 3 lattice. Panel (a) shows the time-dependent autocorrelation function in the full Hilbert space (FHS), panel (b) displays the corresponding lattice, and panel (c) presents the autocor- relation function in the single-particle case. The optimization is performed at the FHS level by minimizing the cost function defined in Eq. (4).

FIG. 3. Illustration of the effective connectivity of a 5 × 5 lattice to second order in perturbation theory. In addition to nearest-neighbor couplings, second-order processes generate effective next-nearest-neighbor connections, allowing nodes without a direct link to be connected via two-step hopping paths. For clarity, the graph represents the effective connec- tivity induced by these higher-order processes and does not correspond to the bare geometry of the underlying square lat- tice (see text).

FIG. 4. Time evolution of the autocorrelation function (Eq. (2)) for a 7 × 7 lattice with on-site energies Wi in the single- particle case. Panel (a) shows the time-dependent autocorrelation function for the optimized lattice obtained by minimizing the cost function defined in Eq. (3), while panel (a.2) displays the corresponding lattice configuration. Panel (b) presents the autocorrelation function obtained from the perturbative optimization based on Eq. (15), and panel (b.2) shows the associated lattice configuration.
